coredata.dc:title: N,S-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for palladium(II) ions via Förster resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Dong
Li, Mingzhong
Xu, Hu
Yu, Jun
Wang, Yuhong
Zhang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: The wide use of palladium in chemical industry may lead to the serious environmental issue. In this work, we reported a green light emitted nitrogen-sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-GCDs) as a fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive detection of palladium ions (Pd2+) in photoluminescence quenching via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Under optimal conditions, the as-prepared N,S-GCDs exhibited a good linear relationship between fluorescence response and Pd2+ concentration ranging from 0 to 150 µM with a detection limit of 0.12 µM. Furthermore, the N,S-GCDs probe has also been used to detect Pd2+ in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Palladium ions detection
Förster resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Pd2+ based on N,S-GCDs
Pd2+ detection by N,S-GCDs in real samples

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21808142).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum of the as-prepared N,S-GCDs (A); XPS full-scan spectrum of N,S-GCDs (B) are and the high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (C), N 1s (D), O 1s (E), and S 2p (F), respectively.
XRD pattern of N,S-GCDs (A), TEM of N,S-GCDs (the inset stands for the corresponding HRTEM) (B) and raman spectrum of N,S-GCDs (C).

PL spectra of N,S-GCDs in the presence of increasing amount of Pd2+ (A, the inset represents the relationship between F/F0 and Pd2+ concentration); UV–vis, excitation (λem = 520 nm), and PL spectra (λex = 420 nm) of N,S-GCDs as well as UV–vis spectrum of PdCl2 solution (B); Observed (solid square) and corrected (open square) fluorescence quenching efficiency of Pd2+ toward N,S-GCDs PL (C). Nomalized PL decay curves of N,S-GCDs before (open circle) and after (open square) adding Pd2+ (D, the red and blue curves represent the corresponding fitting curves). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Linear plot of relative PL response (F/F0) of N,S-GCDs as a function of Pd2+ concentration (λex = 420 nm) (A, the error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements); Relative PL response (F/F0) of N,S-GCDs in the presence of different cation (each at 10 mM) (B, blank stands for the test containing only N,S-GCDs without any cation and the insets correspond to the fluorescent photos); The effect of co-existence of other cations on Pd2+ detection by N,S-GCDs. Bar a represents the co-existence of other ions (each at 10 mM) without Pd2+; bar b only corresponds to Pd2+ (100 µM); bar c exhibits the PL response of N,S-GCDs with Pd2+ (100 µM) together with co-existence of other cations (each at 10 mM) (C, the insets correspond to the fluorescent photos).
Schematic illustrations of synthesis and Pd2+ detection of N,S-GCDs.
Comparison of different analysis methods for Pd2+ detection.
Pd2+ Detection performance of N,S-GCDs in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: N,S-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for palladium(II) ions via Förster resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Applied Catalysis, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Applied Catalysis, School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai ChemPartner Co. Ltd, Shanghai 201203, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai ChemPartner Co. Ltd, Shanghai 201203, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The wide use of palladium in chemical industry may lead to the serious environmental issue. In this work, we reported a green light emitted nitrogen-sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-GCDs) as a fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive detection of palladium ions (Pd2+) in photoluminescence quenching via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Under optimal conditions, the as-prepared N,S-GCDs exhibited a good linear relationship between fluorescence response and Pd2+ concentration ranging from 0 to 150 µM with a detection limit of 0.12 µM. Furthermore, the N,S-GCDs probe has also been used to detect Pd2+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the unique physicochemical properties, palladium has been widely used in the fields of catalysis [1], alloy materials [2], hydrogen storage [3], fuel cells [4], dental materials [5], and surgical utensils etc [6]. The wide application inevitably leads to the introduction of palladium ions (Pd2+) into the ecosystem even food chain, which will impose the serious threat to human being and environment [7]. For example, Pd2+ can interact with amino acids containing thiols, which will destroy proteins and further lead to DNA degradation or a series of symptoms such as allergic reactions [8]. Therefore, developing a sensitive and selective detection of Pd2+ in environmental or biological samples is urgently desired. Traditionally, there are many analytical methods for Pd2+, including X-ray fluorescence [9] (XRF), atomic absorption spectroscopy [10] (AAS), atomic emission spectroscopy [11] (AES), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry [12] (ICP-AES), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [13] (ICP-MS), and solid phase microextraction coupled high performance liquid chromatography [14] (SPME-HPLC). However, these methods usually require the relatively complicated pre-treatment process (time consuming) as well as the high cost for instrumental maintenance or operation, which limits greatly their effective on-site detection application. In contrast, fluorescence analysis method has many advantages such as simplicity, easy operation, high sensitivity, and low cost etc. To date, lots of chemosensors for Pd2+ using organic fluorophores including rhodamine B [15], fluorescein [16], naphthalimide [17], boron-dipyrromethane (BODIPY) [18], and cyanine dye [19] as optical signal resource have been developed. However, the tedious synthesis and purification of these organic fluorophores as well as their poor photostability and bio-compatibility hinder their real application and research.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) as a carbon-based nanomaterial is frequently applied in fluorescence analysis owing to the outstanding properties such as easy access, excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties, high chemical stability, good water solubility, low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility etc [20–22]. Fluorescent CDs has been developed to probe selectively various analytes in water and biological samples, such as metal ion [20,23], anions [24], amino acid [25], bio-enzymes [26], and protein [27] etc. However, as far as we know, few articles have been reported for the detection of Pd2+ by fluorescent CDs [28–31]. For instance, Gao [28] et al. reported a red light emitted CDs as a fluorescent probe for noble metal ions including Pd2+ via PL on–off mode. More recently, Dong et al. [31] developed a blue light emitted CDs to detect Pd2+ and iron(III) ions by PL quenching mode. However, their relatively poor sensitivity [28,31] and linear range [30] are always the obstacles for real applications. In addition, the blue PL of CDs [29,31] also limits the application in biological fields because of the relatively poor tissue penetration depth.

To further develop the noble metal Pd2+ detection by fluorescent CDs, herein, we reported a nitrogen/sulfur co-doped green light emitted CDs (N,S-GCDs) as a fluorescent probe for the determination of Pd2+ (Scheme 1 ). N,S-GCDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal route using ethylenediamine and 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) as the starting materials. The as-prepared N,S-GCDs exhibited strong green emission at 520 nm upon excitation at 420 nm. In the presence of Pd2+, N,S-GCDs exhibited a PL quenching phenomenon and the extent of PL quenching related linearly the concentration of Pd2+. The N,S-GCDs probe for Pd2+ exhibited a limit of detection (LOD) down to 0.12 µM. The specific detection performance of N,S-GCDs was also verified in real water samples and the satisfied results were obtained.

Ethylenediamine (99%), DTNB (99%), anhydrous iron(III) chloride (FeCl3), ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), copper(II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), silver(I) nitrate (AgNO3), disodium hydrogenorthophosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogenorthophosphate (NaH2PO4), palladium(II) chloride (PdCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), barium nitrate (Ba(NO3)3), strontium nitrate (Sr(NO3)3), cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)3·6H2O), calcium chloride (CaCl2), Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), mercury(II) nitrate hydrate (1 mg·mL−1 Hg in 10% HNO3) chromium(III) nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. All these reagents were of analytical grade without further purification. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM) was prepared by Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. Deionized distilled water was used throughout.

UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were recorded on 759S spectrophotometer and F97pro fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively (Leng Guang, Shanghai). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were performed on a JEOL JEM-2100 at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. TEM samples were prepared by depositing a drop of dilute suspension of nanoparticles on a copper grid coated with ultra-thin carbon film. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was measured using a Rigaku Ultimate IV (Japan). The raman spectrum was performed on a Thermo Fischer DXR (USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a NICOLET IZ10 spectrometer with potassium bromide (KBr) pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on a GENESIS 60S (EDAX). Fluorescence lifetime was recorded on a LifeSpec-ps spectrometer (FLS920, Edinburgh).

N,S-GCDs were prepared by hydrothermal method according to the previous method [32]. Briefly, 1.16 mmol of DTNB (0.4597 g) and ethylenediamine (7.2 mL) were added into 40 mL deionized distilled water. Then the solution was sonicated for 20 min. And the solution was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (YZPR-100ML) and heated to 200 °C for 18 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, a dark brown solution was obtained. The larger particles in crude solution were removed with a 0.22 µm filter, and then the dark brown solution was concentrated to ca. 3–5 mL under reduced pressure. The dark solution was further loaded on a silica gel column to collect the green emission band component by using water as the eluent. The purified N,S-GCDs was further dissolved in appropriate amount of pure water to give the mother solution (0.01 mg·mL−1), which was stored at 4 °C for further use. It is noted that we can modulate the emission wavelength of the resulting CDs by regulating the thermal-pyrolysis temperature, ratio of reactants (DTNB/ethylenediamine), and heating time.

PL measurements were performed under the same conditions for all experiments: The slit widths for the excitation and emission tests were set at 10 nm. Upon excitation at 420 nm PL emission was collected in the range of 450–700 nm. PBS (10 mM) with different pH values were used to maintain the constant pH values in test samples unless otherwise statement. The typical Pd2+ detection procedure: Under stirring, transfer 10 µL of N,S-GCDs mother solution to 4 mL of PBS solution (pH 8.0, 10 mM) and record the PL intensity of this suspensions (setting as F0). Then, appropriate concentration of Pd2+ was added into the above N,S-GCDs suspension and the PL intensity (F) is recorded immediately. Changing the Pd2+ concentrations can obtain a series of F/F0 values.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared N,S-GCDs was firstly characterized by FTIR. The broad peak in the range from 3400 to 3000 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH, implying the presence of large amounts of hydroxyl and amino functional groups on N,S-GCDs surface. The signals at 2926, 1038, and 826 cm−1 were assigned to CH stretching vibration [33], CH in-plane deformation, and CH deformation vibration of aromatic ring, respective. The peaks at 1397 and 1512 cm−1 could be assigned to the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching of nitro group, respectively. Meanwhile, the peak at 618 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of CS bond [34]. The band at 1668 cm−1 belongs to the characteristic CO vibration in amide group showing the successful formation of amide groups during preparation process [35]. While the characteristic peak at 1174 and 1079 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CN bond in amide group. Moreover, The above information provide evidence about the existence of OH, CS, CN, NO2, NH2 and C(O)NR groups on the surface of N,S-GCDs.

Then the surface component of N,S-GCDs was further investigated by XPS technique. The full XPS spectrum of N,S-GCDs in Fig. 1B revealed that the sample was mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur elements. The C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p signals corresponded to the binding energy values of 284.4, 399.2, 531.2, and 163.4 eV, respectively [36]. The high-resolved spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1C) showed three peaks centered at 287.8, 285.4, and 284.5 eV standing for CO, CC and C(O)R/CS/CN bonds, respectively [37]. The high resolution spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 1D) could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.2 and 400.2 eV, which represented NH and CN/SN, respectively. The high resolution spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 1E) exhibited two peaks at 531.1 and 532.4 eV, which corresponded to OH and CO, respectively [38]. The high resolution spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 1F) could be deconvoluted into two bands at 164.3 and 163.3 eV, which represented S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 positions of CS bond [39]. These XPS results together with FTIR analysis confirm that the as-prepared CDs are nitrogen and sulfur containing, carbon-rich nanodots modified with hydroxyl, amino, amide, and carbonyl groups etc. It is beneficial for CDs to facilitate the redshift of emission wavelength and improve the emission efficiency by doping N and S elements [40].

The as-synthesized N,S-GCDs was also investigated by XRD analysis. Compared to the standard pattern of graphite carbon (ca. 26.7°), N,S-GCDs showed a broad and intensive peak at ca. 22.9° (Fig. 2 A), which could be attributed to the disordered carbon atoms with a high degree of C(002) planes for hexagonal graphite structure [41]. The broadened XRD peak of N,S-GCDs might be due to the relatively small sized nanoparticles. The structural characterization of N,S-GCDs was further investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2B, the as-prepared N,S-CQD was spherical (5.4 ± 0.5 nm), uniform, and well dispersed indicating no significant aggregation. The HRTEM image showed a clear lattice structure showing high crystallinity of N,S-GCDs. Until now, the precise control of CDs’sizebelow 5 nm is still difficult to implement. Generally, changing growth time is an important means to regulate the particle size and optical performance of quantum dot.[42] Yuan et al. [43] developed multicolored CDs with controllable size from 1.9 to 3.9 nm in the presence of H2SO4 as catalyst by means of varying heating time. In our case, we also obtained the larger CDs with average diameter of ca. 12 nm by extending reaction time to 48 h (Fig. S1). Raman analysis is also one of powerful tools for the characterization of carbon related nanomaterials. As shown in Fig. 2C, N,S-GCDs exhibited two bands at 1574 and 1359 cm−1, which could be attributed to sp2 hybridized carbon (G-band) and sp3 hybridized carbon (D-band), respectively [44]. D-band is usually related to vibrations of carbon atoms of dangling bonds in termination plane of disordered graphite. While G-band is often ascribed to the E2g mode of graphite, which can be related to the vibration of sp2 carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The intensity ratio of D-band and G-band (ID/IG) is usually used to characterize the defect density in carbon material [45]. The ratio less than 1.0 suggests the well graphitized carbon materials. In our case, ID/IG was found to be 0.86 indicating the less defect states in N,S-GCDs. All these results demonstrated that the as-synthesized N,S-GCDs possess a graphitic structure without too many surface defects. These results implied that our N,S-GCDs have the most fundamental features as a promising fluorescent probe material.

The stability of N,S-GCDs has been further evaluated by recording the PL profiles. As shown in Fig. S2A, N,S-GCDs solution could be stored for 180 days in dark without evident change in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity of N,S-GCDs remained almost constant in the presence of varying NaCl concentration (0.0–1.0 M), which indicated that N,S-GCDs exhibited a strong tolerance to high ionic strength (Fig. S2B). The photostability of N,S-GCDs was also investigated under sunlight. During the test period (300 min), no significant change in PL intensity could be observed (Fig. S2C). The thermostability of N,S-GCDs at different temperature (10–90 °C) was also tested. Under all tested temperatures, N,S-GCDs still displayed the relatively strong PL efficiency (Fig. S2D). All these results demonstrated that the as-prepared of N,S-GCDs exhibited the excellent chemical stability, photostability, and thermostability.

As a fluorescent nanomaterial, N,S-GCDs emitted a strong green emission (λem = 520 nm) upon excitation at 420 nm. The PL quantum yield (Φ F) of N,S-GCDs was found to be about 0.16 by using sodium fluorescein as a reference (Φ F = 0.92 in H2O) [44] (see Supplementary Materials). Like other common CDs, its emission exhibited almost the independent on the excitation wavelength (Fig. S3). Moreover, N,S-GCDs showed a relatively high photostability since no significant PL loss was observable even after storing at ambient circumstance for half a year (Fig. S2A). Generally, the PL of CDs is significantly influenced by their surface functional groups or structures. Once an obvious change in surface structures or circumstance occurs, the corresponding optical response will be observed. For instance, Zong et al. [46] reported a CDs-based probe for Cu2+ via PL quenching mode caused by electron transfer. In this case, N,S-GCDs was really inert to Cu2+, but was substantially sensitive to Pd2+, which triggered an evident PL quenching. When the aliquots of Pd2+ solution were added into N,S-GCDs suspension (2.5 × 10-5 mg·mL−1), the PL intensity decreased gradually retaining the original PL shape and position (Fig. 3 A). This result implied that higher Pd2+ concentration could induce more effectively the quenching of N,S-GCDs PL. In addition, the PL response of N,S-GCDs to Pd2+ was really sensitive and the PL quenching could be completed within several seconds (Fig. S4).

Typically, the possible PL quenching of CDs can be photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) etc. As for the PL quenching of N,S-GCDs upon adding Pd2+, there was a large extent of spectra overlap between excitation/emission of N,S-GCDs and absorption of Pd2+ (Fig. 3B), implying the possibility of IFE or FRET processes. The fluorescence lifetime was a good factor to distinguish IFE or FRET quenching. There is an evident decrease trend of fluorescence lifetime of donor in FRET process, while the lifetime of donor remains relatively constant in IFE process. In this case, the excited state of N,S-GCDs decayed exponentially with an average lifetime of 4.02 ns by monitoring the emission at 520 nm. However, the lifetime was decreased dramatically to 0.69 ns upon adding Pd2+ (Fig. 3C). This significant shorten exciton lifetime of N,S-GCDs implied that the intrinsic recombination of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs of N,S-GCDs were significantly suppressed by Pd2+, which directly eliminated the possibility of IFE process. Certainly, the third condition should be also met for FRET mechanism, i.e. the distance between N,S-GCDs and Pd2+ falling in the range of 10–100 Å. The N,S-GCDs/Pd2+ distance was estimated to be about 26.6 Å from the lifetime decay data according to previous method (see Supplementary Materials) [47], which located in the 10–100 Å. Thus, we had good reasons for stating that the PL quenching of N,S-GCDs by Pd2+ was mainly ascribed to the FRET from N,S-GCDs to Pd2+ with high energy transfer efficiency (0.83) though we did not rule out the possibility of PET process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pd2+ based on N,S-GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the N,S-GCDs can be developed to a fluorescent probe for Pd2+. Then, we studied the pH effect on Pd2+ detection by N,S-GCDs. Due to the displaying stability of N,S-GCDs in pH range from 4.0 to 9.0 (Fig. S5A). We attempted to investigate the pH effect on Pd2+ detection performance of N,S-GCDs at range of pH 4.0–9.0. It was found that the best PL response F/F0 was at pH 7.0 (Fig. S5B). Too low pH could lead to the weaker PL response due to the poor stability of N,S-GCDs itself (Fig. S5A). On the other hand, too high pH value was not beneficial for Pd2+ detection owing to the formation of metal hydroxides or oxides. From the accurate tests of pH effect, pH 7.0 was selected to use in the subsequent detection.

Specific detection performance of N,S-GCDs for Pd2+ was further evaluated. Under the optimized conditions, F/F0 linearly correlated to the Pd2+ concentration range of 0–150 μM with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9965 as shown in Fig. 4 A. According to the IUPAC 3σ criterion, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.12 µM. The LOD values are comparable with the previous reports (Table 1 ). The relative standard deviations (RSD) from three independent measurements with 20, 60, and 100 µM of Pd2+ were found to be 2.38, 2.54, and 2.85, respectively. The results indicate the relatively good repeatability. The moderate sensitivity of N,S-GCDs for Pd2+ can be attributed to the high FRET efficiency as well as the relatively high Φ F of N,S-GCDs.

To demonstrate the detection performance of N,S-GCDs for Pd2+, the optical response of N,S-GCDs to other common cations (Ca2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Sr2+) was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4B, N,S-GCDs maintained a relatively strong PL intensity even if the concentration of these ions was 100 times higher than that of Pd2+. However, the presence of Pd2+ with a low concentration (100 µM) could effectively trigger an evident PL quenching. To further verify the genuine Pd2+ detection ability of N,S-GCDs, the selectivity was also evaluated under the co-presence of other cations and the results were illustrated in Fig. 4C. The experimental results indicated that in N,S-GCDs suspension, the co-presence of common cations (each at 10 mM) did not produce significant PL quenching and the solution still displayed a green emission (bar a in Fig. 4C). We also conducted the test for Pd2+ without the co-existence of these interfering cations for comparison (bar b in Fig. 4C). Moreover, the test containing Pd2+ and the co-existence cation was also carried out (bar c in Fig. 4C). These results suggested that N,S-GCDs probe showed a high selectivity to Pd2+. Even the co-existence of other cations imposed a negligible effect on Pd2+ detection. Although it was reported that transition metal ions such as Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ etc. could significantly quench the PL of CDs [46,52,53] our results indirectly demonstrated that our N,S-GCDs as a fluorescent probe exhibited the excellent selectivity to Pd2+ over other common cations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pd2+ detection by N,S-GCDs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practical performance of N,S-GCDs for Pd2+, the determination of Pd2+ in real samples was also conducted. Tap water (Fengxian district in Shanghai), river water (Xinnong river flowing through the new campus of Shanghai Institute of Technology in Fengxian district), sea water (East China Sea, Hangzhou Bay), and cell cultured media DMEM (1:3 diluted by PBS) were used as the real samples as well as the carriers for N,S-GCDs probe. These aqueous samples were firstly filtrated by 0.22 μm membrane to remove larger particles. Then, the samples were diluted by PBS buffer (pH 7.0, sample to PBS volume ratio equal to 1:1) to maintain the optimal pH circumstance. Direct Pd2+ analysis by N,S-GCDs did not lead to the evident PL quenching, which indicated that the origin samples did not contain any Pd2+ or contained undetectable Pd2+ content. Thus, the Pd2+ contents in real samples were artificially regulated by dropping standard Pd2+ stock solution and verified by the proposed method. The experimental results were summarized in Table 2 and were in good agreement with the added. As we known that many cations such as Na+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and Zn2+ etc. might be present in drinking water. The maximum contamination level for some harmful components, like Cu2+, can be present at a high level of 1.3 mg·L-1 (ca. 20 μM). In addition, DMEM contains many nutrients including glucose and amino acids, which can guarantee the nutrition supply. However, our results demonstrated in real samples verified that the presence of these common cations and nutrients did not induce the interfere to Pd2+ detection by N,S-GCDs. This also demonstrated indirectly the promising practicability of our proposed probe for Pd2+ in aqueous media.

In summary, we have reported a fluorescent probe N,S-GCDs for Pd2+ detection by PL quenching via FRET. The excellent selectivity and sensitivity of N,S-GCDs to Pd2+ are ascribed to the relatively high PL quantum yield (0.16) and the high FRET efficiency (0.83). This probe has been used in real water samples and biological medium. The further research alone the application of N,S-GCDs in biological imaging is under way.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21808142).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rechem.2021.100179.
